title: Chapter 11: Reconnaissance
content: Derek didn't come knocking. That worried me more than if he had.

A man who throws a tantrum—shouting, shooting, chasing—is a man who's lost control. Predictable. Manageable. You can plan around noise. But a man who goes quiet after being humiliated? That's a man who's *thinking*. And a thinking enemy was infinitely more dangerous than a loud one.
I expressed this concern to Luna over breakfast on Day Nine, while Sarah slept on the couch with Boss curled against her stomach.

"He's planning something," I said, breaking a cracker in half and dipping it in the last of the peanut butter. "He's not going to just let Sarah walk out of his life. Not Derek. Not a guy who calls women *property*."

Luna considered this with the grave attention she gave all strategic discussions. Her vocabulary had expanded to roughly forty words now, and she could construct simple sentences—subject, verb, object—but complex reasoning still required patience and creative gesturing.
"Man is... small," she said, struggling for the word. "Inside. Small inside. Big body, small inside. Small men... hold grudges."
I blinked. "That's... actually a really good insight."

"Virologist," she said simply, as if that explained it. And maybe it did—she'd been someone who understood systems, patterns, the way tiny things could have outsized effects. The virus might have taken her memories, but it hadn't taken her analytical mind.
"We need to know what he's doing," I said. "How much ammo he has left. Whether he's fortified his position. Whether he's... talked to anyone else."

The last point was critical. Crimson Ridge had two hundred units. I'd accounted for maybe thirty—dead, undead, or cleared. That left a lot of closed doors, and behind any of them could be survivors who'd chosen silence. Derek was charismatic in a thuggish way, and he had supplies to trade. If he found other survivors—particularly other armed survivors—our titanium door might not be enough.
"I go," Luna said. "Listen. Watch. Come back."
"The hallway zombies—"
"Dead things. Slow. Stupid." She held up her hand and flexed her fingers, demonstrating the casual strength that could crumple steel. "I am different."

She was. She'd been different since the beginning—faster, smarter, more coordinated than any zombie I'd encountered. The system called her recovery "abnormal." I was starting to think she'd never been a normal zombie at all.
"Okay," I said. "But be careful. If Derek sees you—"

"He won't." No arrogance. Just fact. Luna moved through the building like smoke through a keyhole—she'd passed Derek twice during our Floor 8 operation, and he'd never spotted her even when she was standing five feet away.

She left through the stairwell, slipping past the barricade with liquid grace, and I was alone with a sleeping Sarah and a cat who regarded me with the judgmental stare that only orange tabbies could muster.
Sarah woke at noon. I knew she was awake because I heard her stop breathing—the specific silence of someone pretending to be asleep, then giving up the pretense.
"Hey," I said from the kitchen. "There's beans. And I found a granola bar in the vending machine stash—it's crushed, but it's edible."

She appeared in the kitchen doorway, her hair a mess, her bruised face pale in the afternoon light. She'd washed up this morning—I'd left water and a clean towel out, deliberately not offering to help because I'd seen the way she flinched when men got too close. The split lip was healing. The bruises were yellowing at the edges.
"Where's Luna?" she asked.
"Scouting. She's checking the building."
"She just... walks around? With all those things out there?"
"She's... immune doesn't feel like the right word. They don't attack her. I think they see her as one of them."
Sarah absorbed this. "She was one of them."
"She *is* one of them. Partially. She's getting better, but she's still..." I struggled for the word. "Transitional. Not human, not zombie. Something in between."
"That's terrifying."
"Yeah. It is. But she's on our side."

"Is she?" Sarah's eyes were sharp—pre-med sharp, the diagnostic gaze of someone trained to see beneath surfaces. "She's territorial about you. I saw how she looked at me when we came in. Like I was... competition."

The observation landed with uncomfortable precision. Luna had been distant with Sarah—not hostile, but not welcoming either. She'd defer to me in Sarah's presence, pressing closer than usual, her body language a quiet declaration of *mine*.
"She's protective," I said. "She's never had to share before."
"She's jealous." Sarah said it flatly, no judgment. "That's different from protective. Protective means she wants you safe. Jealous means she wants you *hers*. And those aren't the same thing."
I opened my mouth to argue, then closed it. She wasn't wrong.
"I'll talk to her," I said.
"Good. Because I'm not going to be the reason your zombie girlfriend feels threatened. I've had enough of territorial men to last several apocalypses."
The jab landed harder than she probably intended. I deserved it.

Luna returned at dusk. I heard her before I saw her—the faint scrape of bare feet on the stairwell, so quiet that only my now-trained ears caught it. She slipped through the barricade, through the hallway, and through the titanium door with the ease of someone who'd mapped every shadow.
"Report," I said, and she gave me a look that suggested she'd learned that word from too many war movies.
"Floor 8," she began, settling into the couch armchair that had become her spot. "Derek is... building. Moving furniture. Barricading stairwell door. He has..."
She held up two fingers.
"Two what? Guns?"
"Two... people. New people. Man and woman. Came from outside. He let them in."
My stomach dropped. "He's recruiting."
"Trading. Food for... loyalty." Luna's lip curled with the particular disgust she reserved for betrayal. "They follow him now. Three against two."
Three against two—Derek plus two new followers versus me and Luna. And Sarah was a non-combatant, more liability than asset in a fight. The math had just gotten significantly worse.
"Are they armed?"
"The man has a knife. The woman has nothing. She looks..." Luna paused, searching for the word. "Scared. Like Sarah was."
Another woman in danger. Another rescue waiting to happen.

**SIGNAL DETECTED — Floor 8**
**NON-ROMANCE TARGET: Jessica Park, Status: Endangered**
**NOTE: Not a romance target. Rescue is optional. System does not reward non-romance interventions.**

The system was telling me, in its clinical way, that Jessica Park wasn't my problem. No quest. No reward. No titanium upgrade. Just a scared woman with a man who'd already proven he was willing to hurt people.

I looked at Sarah, who was listening from the couch with an expression I'd come to recognize: the particular stillness of someone watching a decision being made that they understood on a bone-deep level.
"If you're thinking about saving her," Sarah said quietly, "you should know that it's not your responsibility. You can't save everyone."
"I know."
"But you're going to try anyway."
I looked at Luna. At the cat. At the woman on my couch who'd said "please don't" and meant it with every broken piece of herself.
"I can't save everyone," I said. "But I can try for one more."
Sarah closed her eyes. Not in defeat—in something that looked, from the right angle, like hope.
"Together," Luna said.
There was that word again. I was starting to think it meant more than I'd realized.
"Together," I agreed.
